executive_summary: Speedgoat scores 59/100 — a company whose product quality and customer proof (CERN, GE Aerospace, Mobileye, MIT) far outpace its marketing execution. The single most urgent gap is conversion infrastructure: repeated 'Learn more' CTAs, a 250-field contact form with no response SLA, and zero pricing signals are leaving qualified engineers on the table every day. The top three actions — CTA rewrites, contact form redesign, and schema markup — require minimal budget and could add an estimated $45,000–$120,000/month in pipeline within 90 days.

findings.finding: Conversion score 34/100 — 'Learn more' is the dominant CTA across the entire homepage. No specificity, no urgency, no stage-differentiation. For a $10k–$100k+ hardware purchase, this is the lowest-converting CTA possible.

Contact form leads with a 250-option country dropdown (first field), offers three ambiguous paths (Quote/Config/Support) with no routing guidance, and states zero response time SLA. The highest-intent page on the site has no trust signals adjacent to the form.

Zero schema markup across the entire site. Product, Event, Article, HowTo, BreadcrumbList, and Organization schema are all absent — the site is ineligible for any Google rich results despite having 70+ case studies, a full events calendar, and a deep Knowledge Center.
/about page returns a confirmed 404. Internal and external links pointing to this URL are discarding PageRank into a dead end. Immediate 301 redirect to /company is required.

No pricing signals anywhere on the site — no pricing page, no 'starting at' anchor, no tier names. This forces every budget-curious visitor (including academic buyers and startup engineers) into a sales conversation they may not be ready for.

No hreflang implementation despite offices in Switzerland, USA, and Germany. Google may be serving incorrect regional/language content across all three markets. No German-language content exists for the DE/CH markets.

The best differentiating sentence on the site — 'Exclusively designed for Simulink Real-Time' — appears on the contact page, not the homepage hero. Meanwhile, the homepage leads with 'Real-Time Simulation and Testing,' which any competitor could claim.

70+ customer success stories (including CERN, GE Aerospace, Mobileye, AMZ Racing) are siloed in a dedicated section. No customer logos, testimonials, or quantified outcomes appear on the homepage or product pages where they would have 3–5x the conversion impact.

Zero competitive comparison content. No 'Speedgoat vs. dSPACE' page, no alternatives content, no comparison tables. Engineers searching 'dSPACE alternative Simulink' find no Speedgoat-authored answer — this is high-intent traffic being lost to third-party forums.

No named founders or executives visible on the site. For an 18-year-old company founded by ex-MathWorks engineers, leadership anonymity is a material trust deficit with enterprise procurement teams and research institutions.

quick_wins: Replace every 'Learn more' CTA with outcome-specific copy: product pages → 'See Technical Specs', success stories → 'Read the Case Study', services → 'Talk to an Application Engineer'.
Add 'We respond within 1 business day' immediately below the contact form submit button — the single highest-ROI friction reducer available, costs nothing to implement.
301-redirect /about → /company immediately. Check Google Search Console for inbound links to /about before redirecting to preserve any external PageRank.
Move 'Exclusively designed for Simulink Real-Time' from the contact page to the homepage hero section — the strongest differentiating sentence on the site is in the wrong place.
Add a horizontal trust bar (ISO badge + MathWorks logo + '70+ global customers' + 'Since 2007') directly below the homepage hero CTA and above the contact form submit button.
Add one attributed customer quote to the contact page — peak intent is peak anxiety; one quote from a named engineer at a recognizable company converts at the highest rate per word of copy.
Implement Organization schema on the homepage and BreadcrumbList schema on all case study and article pages — zero code required beyond JSON-LD blocks, eligible for Google rich results immediately.
medium_term: Redesign the contact form as a two-step progressive experience: Step 1 routes by intent (Quote/Config/Support) with plain-language descriptions; Step 2 collects name, email, message with country auto-populated by IP geolocation.

Create a Pricing & Configuration FAQ page at /pricing — explain the configuration-based model, list typical decision factors, name product tiers, signal academic pricing availability. No need to publish exact prices.
Publish one 'Speedgoat vs. dSPACE' comparison article targeting the high-intent query 'dSPACE alternative Simulink' — written engineer-to-engineer, not a marketing puff piece.
Implement Product, Event, Article, and HowTo schema markup across products, events calendar, Knowledge Center articles, and how-to guides. Run all through Google's Rich Results Test after deployment.

Embed contextually matched success story pull-quotes on each solution and product page directly above the primary CTA — automotive HIL page gets AMZ Racing quote, aerospace page gets GE Aerospace or MBDA reference.
Implement hreflang tags for en-US, de-DE, de-CH, and x-default across all pages. Submit locale-specific sitemaps to Google Search Console for each market.
strategic: Build dedicated industry landing pages for the top 5 verticals (Automotive, Aerospace & Defense, Power Electronics, Medical Devices, Energy) — each optimized for '[industry] HIL testing' queries with vertical-specific certifications, case studies, and terminology.

Launch a YouTube channel repurposing existing Knowledge Center webinars with optimized titles and descriptions. Add a production-light 'HIL in 10 Minutes' explainer series. YouTube content ranks in Google search and creates a second discovery channel at near-zero marginal cost.

Build a Speedgoat Practitioner Community (Discourse forum or Slack) to capture the academic-to-industry flywheel: students who learn Speedgoat at MIT or ETH Zurich should stay connected through a community platform and naturally specify Speedgoat when they join OEMs.

Develop a self-service configuration estimator tool — guided by industry → application type → channel count → interface types → output recommended target machine variant and I/O module starter list with a 'Request this configuration' CTA.

Launch an Academic Flywheel Program with documented pricing signals, curriculum integration resources (Simulink course templates, lab exercises), and a student case study series — modeled on how MATLAB itself achieved market dominance through engineering education standardization.

competitors.name: dSPACE
competitors.positioning: Dominant HIL market leader since 1988; broadest ecosystem; defines the category
competitors.pricing: Fully opaque; typically $20k–$50k+ for entry configurations
competitors.social_proof: 30+ year track record; very strong academic and OEM presence
competitors.content: Deep product docs; limited marketing content; no comparison pages

competitors.name: NI / Emerson
competitors.positioning: Platform breadth; LabVIEW + VeriStand; Emerson acquisition brings enterprise distribution
competitors.pricing: Partial transparency; some NI modules listed; ~$10k–$25k entry
competitors.social_proof: Strong; NI Community is active; established Fortune 500 customer base
competitors.content: Strong developer documentation; Python API support; active online community

competitors.name: OPAL-RT
competitors.positioning: High-fidelity power grid and FPGA-based simulation specialist; strong in energy sector
competitors.pricing: Fully opaque; typically $20k–$60k for entry configurations
competitors.social_proof: Moderate; strong in power systems niche; less automotive visibility
competitors.content: Technical depth; limited marketing content; no comparison or alternatives pages
